sources.notes: Players repeatedly choose an existing value x, score its frequency, and decrement all x to x-1.
Official editorial treats each current frequency block as a pile and always takes the largest available score.

annotation.summary: Compute Alice and Bob's final scores in a game where choosing value x scores its current frequency and moves all x down to x-1.
annotation.constraints: a_i can be large, but only n distinct occupied levels exist; O(n log n) over frequency blocks is fine.
annotation.core_idea: Every move chooses one current block of equal values and scores its size; after the move that block merges into the next lower level. Taking a smaller current block while a larger block is available can only give the opponent the larger score, so optimal play is to repeatedly take the largest current block. A max-heap over level frequencies, with merges into lower levels, simulates the game.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) per test case.
tricks: current equal-value block as a pile
merge block into x-1 after taking

solution_variants.name: max-heap-frequency-game
solution_variants.summary: Maintain current frequencies by value, repeatedly pop the largest score block, assign it to the current player, and merge it downward.

tags.evidence: The problem is an alternating scoring game and requires proving the optimal move order.
The optimal strategy is to always take the currently largest frequency block.
A max-heap implements the repeated choice of the largest current score.
